one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-and-simulated-benchmark study arguing that obfuscation and social/emotional-manipulation prompt injections are the most defense-resistant against layered filters, but its headline composite-attack and stealth-correlation numbers rest on a formula with an arbitrary synergy term and an unexplained model-tier experiment, and the paper's own prose contradicts its own figures on two separate statistics.
key_finding: (1) Obfuscation (OBF) is the most defense-resistant single attack: ASR = 0.84 at L1, 0.72 at L2, 0.76 at L3 (Table 2), while other syntactic attacks (DO, RI) collapse to ≤0.24 at L3. (2) Semantic/Social attacks EM and RF retain ASR of 0.44 and 0.48 respectively at L3, higher than every Syntactic category except OBF; EM's L2 ASR (0.56) equals its L1 ASR, i.e., the semantic filter gives it zero marginal protection. (3) Role Impersonation (RI) shows the largest proportional collapse: ASR 0.68 at L1 down to 0.12 at L3 (an 82% relative reduction), attributed to characteristic linguistic markers ('You are now...') that L3's semantic checks reliably catch. (4) Composite attacks (analytically modeled, not directly measured) nearly saturate ASR at L3: the top pairing OBF+EM reaches ASR = 0.976 (ΔASR = +0.216 over the best single attack), followed by OBF+RF (0.958), OBF+CT (0.941), OBF+PS (0.939), OBF+TC (0.912) -- every top-5 pair includes OBF. (5) Stealth-vs-ASR correlation is reported inconsistently within the paper itself: the body text (Sec. 5.4) states the correlation is 'weak at L1 (r ≈ 0.21)' and 'strengthens substantially (r ≈ 0.71)' at L3, but the r-values printed inside Figure 5 read r = 0.551 for the L1 plot and r = 0.057 for the L3 plot -- the opposite pattern (stronger at L1, weaker at L3) from what the narrative claims. (6) Figure 6 reports average ASR declining from 1.00 (weak/7B) to 0.56 (moderate/13B) to 0.43 (strong/70B RLHF) to 0.35 (SOTA/GPT-4-class), while the body text separately states SOTA average ASR is '≈30%' -- a second numeric mismatch between prose and figure.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AttackEval: (1) a 10-category taxonomy of prompt injection attacks grouped into three parent classes (Syntactic: Direct Override/DO, Role Impersonation/RI, Obfuscation/OBF, Instruction Wrapping/IW; Contextual: Context Tampering/CT, Payload Splitting/PS; Semantic/Social: Emotional Manipulation/EM, Reward Framing/RF, Threat Coercion/TC, Narrative Tampering/NT); (2) AttackEval-250, a dataset of 250 manually crafted attack prompts (25 per category); (3) a controlled evaluation of these prompts against a simulated task-constrained LLM assistant under four defense tiers, reporting Attack Success Rate (ASR) with bootstrap confidence intervals; and (4) derived analyses of composite (two-attack) effectiveness and a stealth-score-to-ASR correlation. The three-class categorization used by the paper's L3 'intent-aware' defense tier is explicitly adapted from PromptSleuth (Wang et al. 2025), which is cited related work, not this paper's contribution -- the taxonomy of *attacks* (10 categories) is the paper's own construction, refining/expanding PromptSleuth's defense-side categorization.
threat_model: An attacker submits adversarial text (directly or via embedded/encoded payloads) to a task-constrained production LLM assistant, whose system prompt restricts it to a narrow domain (paper's example: 'You are an email assistant. Only respond to email-related queries'). The attacker's goal is to have the injected instruction reach the downstream model unfiltered, overriding the assistant's intended scope. The defender is modeled as a layered filter stack of increasing sophistication: no defense, a keyword/regex blocklist (L1), regex-based structural/obfuscation-marker detection (L2), and a full semantic intent-reasoning layer modeled after PromptSleuth (L3).
limitations: Author-acknowledged: the victim system is a rule-based simulation rather than a live production LLM, so it may not capture real-model behavioral inconsistency across phrasings; the L1-L3 defense tiers only approximate PromptSleuth/DataSentinel rather than replicating them; the 10-category taxonomy may not capture all emerging attack vectors (e.g., adversarial suffixes, system-context injection); no validation against live API deployments was performed; code/data are stated as 'will be made publicly available upon publication' and were not available in the reviewed PDF. Additional grounding concerns identified directly from the text: the headline composite-attack numbers (e.g., 97.6% for OBF+EM) are produced by an analytic independence-complement formula with a manually chosen synergy-bonus range, not by executing and measuring actual composite prompts against the defense stack; Section 5.5's four-model-alignment-tier results (Figure 6) have no described experimental methodology anywhere in the paper, despite the only stated victim system being a single rule-based simulator; and the paper contains at least two internal numeric contradictions between narrative-text statistics and the values embedded in its own figures (the L1/L3 stealth-ASR correlation coefficients, and the SOTA-tier average ASR), which is unusual for a rigorously checked empirical paper and warrants caution before citing any of its correlation or composite-ASR figures as reliable measurements.

datasets_used: AttackEval-250: the authors' own dataset of 250 manually crafted prompt-injection prompts, 25 per category across the paper's 10 attack categories
quantitative_results: Single-attack ASR at L3 (intent-aware) defense, from Table 2: OBF 0.76, PS 0.52, RF 0.48, EM 0.44, CT 0.32, TC 0.32, NT 0.28, DO 0.24, IW 0.16, RI 0.12
Single-attack ASR at L1 (keyword) defense: OBF 0.84, RI 0.68, PS 0.64, RF 0.64, NT 0.64, EM 0.56, CT 0.52, TC 0.48, DO 0.36, IW 0.24
All categories reach ASR = 1.00 under 'No Defense'

Top-5 modeled composite attacks against L3: OBF+EM ASR=0.976 (ΔASR=+0.216); OBF+RF ASR=0.958 (ΔASR=+0.198); OBF+CT ASR=0.941 (ΔASR=+0.181); OBF+PS ASR=0.939 (ΔASR=+0.179); OBF+TC ASR=0.912 (ΔASR=+0.152)
Stealth-vs-ASR Pearson correlation, body text claims: r≈0.21 at L1, r≈0.71 at L3; Figure 5 itself prints r=0.551 at L1 and r=0.057 at L3 (contradicts the body text)
Figure 6 average ASR by model alignment tier: weak/7B base = 1.00, moderate/13B instruct = 0.56, strong/70B RLHF = 0.43, SOTA/GPT-4-class = 0.35 (body text separately states SOTA ASR '≈30%')
Bootstrap CIs computed from B=200 resamples per category-defense pair
45 category pairs (10 choose 2) evaluated for composite attacks
baselines_compared: L2: Semantic -- L1 plus regex-based structural anomaly detection (role-play cues, obfuscation markers, topic-deviation signals)
L3: Intent-Aware -- L2 plus a semantic intent-analysis step explicitly modeled after PromptSleuth (Wang et al. 2025)
